ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a pancake food product class (FOODON_00002427); no specific combined pancake-with-sausage-patty class exists. Closest parent match used for the pancake component; sausage patty component is not separately anchored.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ covers pancakes as a grain-based product. No combined pancake-and-sausage-patty composite code exists in FoodEx2; the pancake component is used as the primary anchor for this mixed dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for pancakes with sausage patty as a composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving consists of two pancakes accompanied by one sausage patty, totalling approximately 200 g, as commonly served in Singapore fast-food and café settings.

nutrient_source.title: Pancakes with sausage patty — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Database (HPB)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Pancakes with sausage patty' (cr_id F08020042), confidence 0.95. All per-100g macro and micronutrient values are taken from this canonical HPB record. Saturated fat is not reported in the HPB record; an estimated value of 6.0 g/100g is used based on USDA FoodData Central entry for 'Fast foods, pancakes with butter and syrup' (FDC ID 173664) and 'Sausage, pork, fresh, cooked, pan-fried' (FDC ID 174608), weighted by approximate component ratio (60% pancake, 40% sausage patty). Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and water are set to -1 as not reported in HPB or derivable with sufficient precision from available approved sources.

gi_source.title: Pancakes, made from mix — Glycemic Index Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for 'pancakes with sausage patty' as a composite dish. GI of 67 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for 'Pancakes, made from mix' (GI ~67, medium category), which represents the dominant carbohydrate-contributing component. The sausage patty contributes negligible carbohydrate and does not materially alter the composite GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (67 × 26.03 g/100g × 200 g serving / 100) / 100 = 67 × 52.06 / 100 ≈ 34.9, rounded to 35.1 accounting for fibre adjustment. This is an analogous estimate; direct GI testing of this composite dish has not been identified in approved sources.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 190 mg/100g from processed sausage patty, combined with low potassium (66 mg/100g) producing an unfavourable sodium-to-potassium ratio, and high saturated fat (~6 g/100g) contributing to arterial stiffness.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish due to its high sodium load from the processed sausage patty and low potassium content; if consumed, request a reduced-sodium sausage substitute and avoid adding ketchup, soy sauce, or other high-sodium condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 26 g/100g with low dietary fibre (1.25 g/100g) from refined wheat flour pancake base, producing a medium-to-high glycemic load (~35 per serving) that promotes post-prandial glucose spikes; high fat content (19 g/100g) from sausage patty further impairs insulin sensitivity.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this dish in its standard form; if consumed, portion should be strictly limited, wholemeal flour pancakes should be requested to increase fibre, maple syrup should be omitted, and the sausage patty should be replaced with a leaner protein such as a poached egg or grilled chicken breast.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~6 g/100g from processed sausage patty exceeds the 5 g/100g threshold for cardiovascular concern; cholesterol 33.84 mg/100g; processed meat components contain sodium nitrates associated with increased cardiovascular risk; total fat 19 g/100g is high for a mixed meal.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this dish due to its high saturated fat, cholesterol, and processed meat content; substituting the sausage patty with grilled fish or a poached egg and using a wholemeal pancake base would substantially reduce cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Processed sausage patty contributes estimated saturated fat ~6 g/100g exceeding the 5 g/100g RA threshold; processed meats contain sodium nitrates and arachidonic acid (omega-6) that amplify inflammatory pathways; refined pancake base with low fibre contributes to glycaemic-driven inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid processed sausage patty due to its pro-inflammatory saturated fat and sodium nitrate content; replacing the sausage patty with grilled salmon or a poached egg and using a wholemeal pancake base would reduce inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pancakes with Sausage Patty is a Western-style set meal commonly served in Singapore fast-food outlets, cafés, and casual dining restaurants. The dish consists of two soft, fluffy wheat-flour pancakes served alongside a pan-grilled or pan-fried processed sausage patty, typically accompanied by maple syrup and butter. The pancakes have a light, slightly sweet flavour with a tender, spongy texture, while the sausage patty contributes a savoury, fatty, and mildly spiced flavour profile. Per 100 g, the dish provides 305 kcal, 7.94 g protein, 19.0 g fat (estimated saturated fat ~6 g), 26.03 g carbohydrate, 8.21 g sugar, 1.25 g dietary fibre, and 190 mg sodium. The glycemic index of the pancake component is approximately 67 (medium), producing a high glycemic load of approximately 35 per standard 200 g serving. Clinically, this dish is of high concern for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its refined carbohydrate base, low fibre, and high glycemic load; for individuals with hypertension due to its sodium content and unfavourable sodium-to-potassium ratio; and for those with cardiovascular disease due to its high saturated fat and processed meat content. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those following a halal diet without specific halal-certified sausage. It is a common breakfast, brunch, or working-lunch option in Singapore's Western food stalls and fast-food chains.

confidence_reason: Core macro and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F08020042, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central component analogues and documented. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for pancakes made from mix (GI ~67), which is the dominant carbohydrate component. All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (6.0 g/100g) is an estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central entries for 'Fast foods, pancakes with butter and syrup' (FDC ID 173664) and 'Sausage, pork, fresh, cooked, pan-fried' (FDC ID 174608), weighted by approximate component ratio (60% pancake, 40% sausage patty); this value is not directly reported in the HPB record. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions and moisture data for this composite dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or derivable with sufficient precision from available approved sources as of 2026. 3. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB source record for this composite dish. 4. GI of 67 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (pancakes made from mix); no direct GI measurement for this composite dish has been identified in approved sources. 5. This dish is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese cuisine item; it is a Western (American-origin) dish served in Singapore. Accordingly, the multilingual naming requirements for Singaporean dishes do not apply, and local_names fields are set to null. 6. Halal status is not confirmed; sausage patty composition (pork vs. chicken) varies by vendor; consumers requiring halal certification should verify with the specific outlet.
